content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers Chapter 9

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 9:1 -- "And YHWH spoke" / "saying" / word order / "after they had come out" (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh ... le-tzetam ... le'mor)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-spoke YHWH unto Moses in-the-wilderness of Sinai, in-the-year the-second of-their-going-out from-the-land of Egypt, in-the-month the-first, saying:" |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt, and said:" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt, in the first month, saying:" -- PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (violating binding convention) and renders le'mor as "and said" instead of "saying." EMET uses archaic "unto" and hyper-literal "of-their-going-out." LOGOS reorders the Hebrew clause, placing "in the first month" before "the second year," which inverts the MT's word order (ba-shanah ha-shenit ... ba-chodesh ha-rishon = "in the year the second ... in the month the first"). The consensus follows LOGOS's fluent English while restoring the MT's clause order: "in the second year ... in the first month."

---

### 9:2 -- "shall keep" vs. "Let ... keep" vs. "are to celebrate" (ve-ya'asu ... ha-Pesakh be-mo'ado)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-shall-make the-Sons of Israel the-Passover at-its-appointed-time." |
| LOGOS | "Let the Sons of Israel keep the Passover at its appointed time." |
| PNEUMA | "The Sons of Israel are to celebrate the Passover at its appointed time." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the Sons of Israel shall keep the Passover at its appointed time." -- The Hebrew ve-ya'asu is a vav-consecutive jussive form ("and let them make/keep"). LOGOS's "Let ... keep" captures the jussive well. PNEUMA's "are to celebrate" uses "celebrate" -- an interpretive expansion of the Hebrew asah ("to make/do"), which in sacrificial context means "to prepare/observe" rather than "to celebrate." EMET preserves the conjunction "And" but uses the awkward "shall-make." The consensus preserves "And" per binding convention, uses "shall keep" (combining EMET's "And" with LOGOS's "keep"), and retains "Passover" without the Hebrew article. "Keep" is preferred over "celebrate" because asah ha-Pesakh is a technical cultic expression meaning to perform the Passover ritual, not merely to festively commemorate it.

---

### 9:3 -- "at twilight" vs. "between the evenings" / "ordinances" vs. "regulations" vs. "judgments" (bein ha-arbayim / mishpatav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "On-the-fourteenth day in-the-month the-this, between the-evenings, you-shall-make it at-its-appointed-time; according-to-all its-statutes and-according-to-all its-judgments you-shall-make it." |
| LOGOS | "On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, you shall keep it at its appointed time; according to all its statutes and according to all its ordinances you shall keep it." |
| PNEUMA | "On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, you are to celebrate it at its appointed time. You must observe it according to all its statutes and all its regulations." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, you shall keep it at its appointed time; according to all its statutes and according to all its ordinances you shall keep it." -- (a) bein ha-arbayim: EMET's "between the evenings" is the literal dual construction; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "at twilight," which captures the temporal sense without resolving the ancient dispute. The consensus follows "at twilight." (b) mishpatav: EMET renders "judgments," LOGOS "ordinances," PNEUMA "regulations." The Hebrew mishpat means a ruling or judicial determination. "Ordinances" (LOGOS) preserves the legal register better than "regulations" (modern bureaucratic connotation). (c) PNEUMA restructures into two sentences and uses "You must observe" and "are to celebrate" -- interpretive paraphrases that depart from the MT's single-sentence structure and the verb asah ("make/keep"). (d) PNEUMA drops the second "according to all," flattening the MT's emphatic doubling (ke-khol ... u-khe-khol). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:4 -- "spoke" vs. "told" / "to keep" vs. "to celebrate" (va-yedabber ... la'asot ha-Pesakh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-spoke Moses unto the-Sons of Israel to-make the-Passover." |
| LOGOS | "And Moses spoke to the Sons of Israel to keep the Passover." |
| PNEUMA | "So Moses told the Sons of Israel to celebrate the Passover." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses spoke to the Sons of Israel to keep the Passover." -- PNEUMA uses "So" instead of "And" (the Hebrew vav-consecutive is rendered throughout as "And" per binding convention) and "told" for va-yedabber (the piel of davar, "to speak," not "to tell" which would be va-yomer or va-yagged). PNEUMA again uses "celebrate" for la'asot; see note at v. 2. EMET uses "unto" (archaic). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:5 -- "kept" vs. "made" vs. "celebrated" / obedience formula wording (va-ya'asu ... ke-khol asher tsivvah YHWH et Mosheh ken asu benei Yisra'el)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-made the-Passover in-the-first, on-the-fourteenth day of-the-month, between the-evenings, in-the-wilderness of Sinai; according-to-all which YHWH commanded Moses, so did the-Sons of Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And they kept the Passover in the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, at twilight, in the wilderness of Sinai; according to all that YHWH had commanded Moses, so the Sons of Israel did." |
| PNEUMA | "And they celebrated the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month, at twilight, in the wilderness of Sinai. The Sons of Israel did everything exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they kept the Passover in the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, at twilight, in the wilderness of Sinai; according to all that YHWH had commanded Moses, so the Sons of Israel did." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "celebrated" for va-ya'asu (see note at v. 2). (b) EMET uses "between the evenings" for bein ha-arbayim; the consensus standardizes to "at twilight." (c) PNEUMA restructures the obedience formula into a separate sentence ("The Sons of Israel did everything exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses"), inserting "everything exactly" -- neither word is in the Hebrew. The MT reads ke-khol asher tsivvah YHWH et Mosheh ken asu benei Yisra'el ("according to all that YHWH commanded Moses, so did the Sons of Israel"). (d) EMET omits "month" after "in the first" (ba-rishon can imply "month" from context, but LOGOS supplies it for clarity). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:6 -- "unclean by the dead body of a man" vs. "unclean by a soul of a man" vs. "ritually unclean through contact with a dead body" (teme'im le-nefesh adam)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-there-were men who were unclean by-a-soul of a-man, and-not they-were-able to-make the-Passover on-the-day the-that; and-they-came-near before Moses and-before Aaron on-the-day the-that." |
| LOGOS | "And there were men who were unclean through a dead body of a man, and they could not keep the Passover on that day. And they came before Moses and before Aaron on that day." |
| PNEUMA | "But there were certain men who had become ritually unclean through contact with a dead body and were unable to celebrate the Passover on that day. They came before Moses and Aaron that same day" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And there were men who were unclean by the dead body of a man, and they could not keep the Passover on that day. And they came before Moses and before Aaron on that day." -- (a) The key phrase teme'im le-nefesh adam literally means "unclean by/for a soul of a man." EMET's literal "by a soul of a man" preserves the Hebrew nefesh but is opaque in English. The word nefesh here is used in its secondary sense of "dead person/corpse" (cf. Num 6:6). LOGOS's "through a dead body of a man" and PNEUMA's "through contact with a dead body" both interpret correctly. The consensus uses "by the dead body of a man," preserving the preposition "by" (closer to le-) and retaining "of a man" (adam). (b) PNEUMA opens with "But" instead of "And," adds "certain" and "ritually" (not in Hebrew), uses "celebrate" again, and says "that same day" where MT has simply ba-yom ha-hu ("on that day"). (c) PNEUMA omits the second "before" in "before Moses and before Aaron" (the MT has lifnei Mosheh ve-lifnei Aharon, with the preposition repeated). The consensus follows LOGOS with slight modification.

---

### 9:7 -- "Why should we be kept back" vs. "why shall we be diminished" vs. "Why should we be excluded" / "bring the offering" vs. "present YHWH's offering" (lammah niggara' le-vilti haqriv et qorban YHWH)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "We are unclean by-a-soul of a-man; why shall-we-be-diminished so-as-not to-bring-near the-offering of YHWH at-its-appointed-time in-the-midst of the-Sons of Israel?" |
| LOGOS | "We are unclean through a dead body of a man. Why are we kept back, so as not to bring the offering of YHWH at its appointed time among the Sons of Israel?" |
| PNEUMA | "We are unclean because of contact with a dead body. Why should we be excluded and not be allowed to present YHWH's offering at its appointed time along with the rest of the Sons of Israel?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "We are unclean by the dead body of a man. Why should we be kept back, so as not to bring the offering of YHWH at its appointed time among the Sons of Israel?" -- (a) niggara': EMET's "diminished" preserves the root meaning of gara' ("to diminish/reduce") but is unusual in English for this context. LOGOS's "kept back" and PNEUMA's "excluded" both interpret the niphal as exclusion. "Kept back" is closer to the passive sense and less interpretive than "excluded." The consensus uses "should we be kept back" -- combining PNEUMA's modal "should" with LOGOS's "kept back." (b) PNEUMA adds "and not be allowed to" (expanding le-vilti) and "the rest of" before "the Sons of Israel" (not in MT). (c) PNEUMA uses "present" for haqriv; the Hebrew means "to bring near" (the hiphil of qarav). "Bring" is more literal; "present" is acceptable but adds a formality not in the root. (d) EMET's "in-the-midst of" for be-tokh is literal; LOGOS's "among" and PNEUMA's "along with the rest of" are interpretations. "Among" suffices.

---

### 9:8 -- "Stand still" vs. "Stand" vs. "Wait here" / "I will hear" vs. "I shall hear" vs. "I find out" (imdu ve-eshme'ah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Stand and-I-shall-hear what YHWH shall-command for-you." |
| LOGOS | "Stand still, and I will hear what YHWH will command concerning you." |
| PNEUMA | "Wait here while I find out what YHWH will command concerning you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Stand still, and I will hear what YHWH will command concerning you." -- (a) imdu: EMET renders simply "Stand," LOGOS "Stand still," PNEUMA "Wait here." The Hebrew imperative amad means "to stand (in place)." LOGOS's "Stand still" captures the sense of remaining stationary while awaiting a response. PNEUMA's "Wait here" is a natural English paraphrase but loses the physical posture implied by amad. (b) ve-eshme'ah: EMET/LOGOS preserve "I shall/will hear" for the cohortative of shama'. PNEUMA renders "I find out," which is an interpretive departure -- shama' means "to hear/listen," not "to find out/discover." (c) EMET uses "for you" (lakhem); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "concerning you." The Hebrew lakhem can mean either "for you" or "concerning you"; "concerning" better captures the context. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:9 -- "saying" vs. "and said" (le'mor)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-spoke YHWH unto Moses, saying:" |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH spoke to Moses and said:" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:" -- Same pattern as v. 1. PNEUMA omits "And" and renders le'mor as "and said." The consensus follows LOGOS with "And" preserved.

---

### 9:10 -- "If any man" vs. "Any man, any man" vs. "When any one of you" / "unclean by a dead body" vs. "unclean through a dead body" vs. "unclean through contact with a dead body" / "he shall still keep" vs. "he shall make" vs. "that person may still celebrate" (ish ish ... tame la-nefesh ... ve-asah Pesakh la-YHWH)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Speak unto the-Sons of Israel, saying: 'Any-man, any-man who shall-be unclean by-a-soul or on-a-journey far-away, for-you or-for-your-generations, and-he-shall-make Passover to-YHWH." |
| LOGOS | "Speak to the Sons of Israel, saying, 'If any man of you or of your generations shall be unclean through a dead body, or shall be on a distant journey, he shall still keep the Passover to YHWH." |
| PNEUMA | "Tell the Sons of Israel: When any one of you or your descendants becomes unclean through contact with a dead body, or is away on a distant journey, that person may still celebrate the Passover to YHWH." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Speak to the Sons of Israel, saying: 'If any man of you or of your generations shall be unclean by a dead body, or shall be on a distant journey, he shall still keep the Passover to YHWH." -- (a) ish ish: EMET preserves the emphatic doubling "any man, any man." LOGOS renders "If any man" and PNEUMA "When any one of you." The doubling in Hebrew functions as a universal distributive; "If any man" captures this without the awkward English repetition. (b) PNEUMA uses "Tell" for dabber ("speak") and "descendants" for doroteikhem ("your generations"). "Speak" is the literal rendering; "generations" preserves the Hebrew doroteikhem more faithfully than "descendants." (c) PNEUMA uses "that person may still celebrate" -- "may" introduces permission, while the Hebrew ve-asah is an indicative/jussive ("and he shall make/keep"), indicating obligation not mere permission. (d) EMET's "by a soul" preserves the Hebrew la-nefesh literally but is opaque. The consensus uses "by a dead body" following LOGOS's interpretation of nefesh as corpse.

---

### 9:11 -- "In the second month" vs. "In the month the second" / "they shall keep it" vs. "they shall make it" vs. "They are to celebrate it" (ba-chodesh ha-sheni ... ya'asu oto ... yokhlu oto)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "In-the-month the-second, on-the-fourteenth day, between the-evenings, they-shall-make it; with unleavened-bread and-bitter-herbs they-shall-eat it." |
| LOGOS | "In the second month, on the fourteenth day, at twilight, they shall keep it; with unleavened bread and bitter herbs they shall eat it." |
| PNEUMA | "They are to celebrate it in the second month, on the fourteenth day, at twilight. They must eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "In the second month, on the fourteenth day, at twilight, they shall keep it; with unleavened bread and bitter herbs they shall eat it." -- (a) PNEUMA restructures the verse, front-loading the verb ("They are to celebrate it") and splitting into two sentences. The MT begins with the temporal clause (ba-chodesh ha-sheni) and the consensus preserves this order. (b) "at twilight" preferred over EMET's "between the evenings." (c) "shall keep" preferred over EMET's "shall make" and PNEUMA's "are to celebrate." (d) PNEUMA restructures the eating clause: "They must eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs," reversing the Hebrew clause order (al matzot u-merorim yokhlu oto = "with unleavened bread and bitter herbs they shall eat it"). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:12 -- "leave none of it" vs. "leave of it" vs. "leave any of it" / "they shall keep it" vs. "they shall make it" vs. "They must observe it" (lo yash'iru mimmenu ... ya'asu oto)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "They-shall-not leave of-it until morning, and-a-bone they-shall-not break in-it; according-to-all the-statute of the-Passover they-shall-make it." |
| LOGOS | "They shall leave none of it until morning, and they shall not break a bone of it; according to all the statute of the Passover they shall keep it." |
| PNEUMA | "They are not to leave any of it until morning, nor break any of its bones. They must observe it according to every statute of the Passover." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "They shall leave none of it until morning, and they shall not break a bone of it; according to all the statute of the Passover they shall keep it." -- (a) PNEUMA restructures extensively: "nor break any of its bones" for ve-etzem lo yishberu vo (literally "and a bone they shall not break in it"); "any of its bones" pluralizes the singular etzem ("bone"). (b) PNEUMA uses "They must observe it according to every statute" -- restructuring the Hebrew clause order and substituting "observe" and "every" for the MT's "they shall make it" and "all the statute of." (c) EMET preserves the Hebrew syntax closely but with less natural English. The consensus follows LOGOS, which balances fidelity and readability.

---

### 9:13 -- "refrains from keeping" vs. "ceases to make" vs. "fails to celebrate" / "that soul shall be cut off" vs. "that person must be cut off" / "shall bear his sin" vs. "will bear the consequences of his sin" (ve-khadal la'asot ... ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ... khet'o yissa)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-man who he is clean and-on-a-journey not he-was, and-he-ceases to-make the-Passover -- and-shall-be-cut-off the-soul the-that from-her-people, for the-offering of YHWH not he-brought-near at-its-appointed-time -- his-sin shall-bear the-man the-that." |
| LOGOS | "But the man who is clean and is not on a journey, and refrains from keeping the Passover, that soul shall be cut off from his people, because he did not bring the offering of YHWH at its appointed time; that man shall bear his sin." |
| PNEUMA | "But anyone who is ritually clean and is not on a journey, yet fails to celebrate the Passover -- that person must be cut off from his people, because he did not present YHWH's offering at its appointed time. That person will bear the consequences of his sin." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But the man who is clean and is not on a journey, and refrains from keeping the Passover -- that soul shall be cut off from his people, because he did not bring the offering of YHWH at its appointed time; that man shall bear his sin." -- (a) ve-khadal: EMET "ceases," LOGOS "refrains," PNEUMA "fails." The Hebrew chadal means "to cease/desist/refrain." "Refrains" (LOGOS) captures the deliberate abstention implied. "Fails" (PNEUMA) suggests inability rather than willful omission. (b) PNEUMA adds "ritually" before "clean" (not in Hebrew). (c) "that soul shall be cut off" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew nefesh ("soul"); PNEUMA substitutes "that person." The karet formula in the Torah consistently uses nefesh, and the consensus preserves this. (d) PNEUMA adds "the consequences of" to "bear his sin" -- the Hebrew khet'o yissa is simply "his sin he shall bear," with no additional word for "consequences." (e) PNEUMA opens with "But anyone" while EMET/LOGOS use "the man" (ha-ish). The MT specifies ha-ish ("the man"), not a general pronoun. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:14 -- "sojourner" vs. "stranger" vs. "foreigner residing among you" (ger) / "one statute" wording / "native of the land" vs. "native-born" (ezrakh ha-aretz)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-if shall-sojourn with-you a-stranger and-he-shall-make Passover to-YHWH, according-to-the-statute of the-Passover and-according-to-its-judgment, so he-shall-make; a-statute one shall-be for-you, and-for-the-stranger and-for-the-native of the-land." |
| LOGOS | "And if a sojourner shall sojourn among you and would keep the Passover to YHWH, according to the statute of the Passover and according to its ordinance, so shall he do. You shall have one statute, both for the sojourner and for the native of the land." |
| PNEUMA | "If a foreigner residing among you wishes to celebrate the Passover to YHWH, he must observe it according to the statute and regulation of the Passover. There is to be one law for all of you -- for the foreigner and for the native-born alike." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if a sojourner shall sojourn among you and would keep the Passover to YHWH, according to the statute of the Passover and according to its ordinance, so shall he do. You shall have one statute, both for the sojourner and for the native of the land." -- (a) ger: EMET renders "stranger," LOGOS "sojourner," PNEUMA "foreigner residing among you." The Hebrew ger specifically denotes a resident alien who has attached to the community. "Sojourner" (LOGOS) preserves the etymological connection to the verb gur ("to sojourn") and is the traditional rendering. PNEUMA's expansion adds words not in the text. (b) PNEUMA renders chuqqah achat as "one law" (following the LXX's nomos eis); the MT uses chuqqah ("statute"), not torah ("law"). (c) PNEUMA uses "native-born" for ezrakh ha-aretz; the MT has "native of the land" (ha-aretz retained). LOGOS preserves this. (d) PNEUMA omits "And" at the opening (violating binding convention), adds "wishes to" (not in the Hebrew ve-asah), and says "he must observe it" instead of the MT's "so shall he do" (ken ya'aseh). (e) PNEUMA restructures "according to the statute of the Passover and according to its ordinance" into the abbreviated "according to the statute and regulation of the Passover." The MT has ke-khuqqat ha-Pesakh u-khe-mishpato ("according to the statute of the Passover and according to its ordinance") with two prepositional phrases. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:15 -- "And on the day that the Tabernacle was set up" vs. "And on the day of setting up the Tabernacle" vs. "On the day the Tabernacle was set up" / "the Tent of the Testimony" phrasing / "as it were an appearance of fire" vs. "like an appearance of fire" vs. "the appearance ... was like fire" (u-ve-yom haqim ... le-Ohel ha-Edut ... ke-mar'eh esh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-on-the-day of setting-up the-Tabernacle, covered the-cloud the-Tabernacle, for the-Tent of the-Testimony; and-in-the-evening there-was upon the-Tabernacle like-an-appearance of fire until morning." |
| LOGOS | "And on the day that the tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered the tabernacle, the tent of the testimony, and at evening there was upon the tabernacle as it were an appearance of fire until morning." |
| PNEUMA | "On the day the Tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered the Tabernacle -- the Tent of the Testimony. From evening until morning, the appearance over the Tabernacle was like fire." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And on the day that the Tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered the Tabernacle, the Tent of the Testimony; and at evening there was upon the Tabernacle as it were an appearance of fire until morning." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" at the opening. (b) The Hebrew le-Ohel ha-Edut is rendered "for the Tent of the Testimony" (EMET, preserving le- = "for"), "the tent of the testimony" (LOGOS, treating it as apposition), "the Tent of the Testimony" (PNEUMA, as apposition with dash). The le- preposition here functions appositionally ("namely, the Tent of the Testimony"); the consensus follows LOGOS's appositional rendering. (c) PNEUMA restructures the evening clause entirely: "From evening until morning, the appearance over the Tabernacle was like fire" -- the Hebrew says "and at evening there was upon the Tabernacle ke-mar'eh esh until morning." PNEUMA drops the simile-qualifying ke-mar'eh ("like an appearance of") and says simply "was like fire," losing the careful qualification. The Hebrew does not say fire, but "like the appearance of fire." LOGOS's "as it were an appearance of fire" preserves both the simile (ke-) and the noun (mar'eh, "appearance"). (d) "Tabernacle" is capitalized per convention for Mishkan. The consensus follows LOGOS with capitalization.

---

### 9:16 -- "So it was continually" vs. "So it continued" / "the cloud would cover it" vs. "the cloud covered it" (ken yihyeh tamid / he-anan yekhassennu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "So it-was continually: the-cloud would-cover it, and-an-appearance of fire night." |
| LOGOS | "So it was continually: the cloud covered it, and an appearance of fire by night." |
| PNEUMA | "So it continued: the cloud covered it by day, and an appearance of fire by night." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "So it was continually: the cloud covered it, and an appearance of fire by night." -- (a) PNEUMA adds "by day" -- not in the Hebrew. The MT says simply he-anan yekhassennu ("the cloud would cover it") without specifying "by day." While the implicit meaning includes daytime, the text does not state it. (b) EMET omits "by" before "night" (the Hebrew laylah = "night" without a preposition, but "by night" is standard English). (c) PNEUMA renders tamid as "continued" rather than "continually" -- the Hebrew adverb tamid means "continually/perpetually," and "it was continually" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the adverbial form. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:17 -- "whenever the cloud was taken up" vs. "according to the going up of the cloud" vs. "whenever the cloud lifted" / "would set out" vs. "would journey" / "settled" vs. "would dwell" (u-le-fi he'alot he-anan ... yis'u ... yishkon ... yakhanu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-according-to the-going-up of the-cloud from-upon the-Tent, and-after-that would-journey the-Sons of Israel; and-in-the-place where would-dwell there the-cloud, there would-encamp the-Sons of Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And whenever the cloud was taken up from over the tent, after that the Sons of Israel would set out; and in the place where the cloud settled, there the Sons of Israel would encamp." |
| PNEUMA | "Whenever the cloud lifted from above the Tent, the Sons of Israel would set out on their journey. And wherever the cloud settled, there the Sons of Israel would make camp." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And whenever the cloud was taken up from over the Tent, after that the Sons of Israel would set out; and in the place where the cloud settled, there the Sons of Israel would encamp." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" at the opening and omits "after that" (ve-akharei khen), which is explicitly in the Hebrew. (b) EMET's "according to the going up" preserves the Hebrew u-le-fi he'alot literally but is awkward. LOGOS's "whenever ... was taken up" captures the habitual sense. (c) PNEUMA adds "on their journey" (not in MT) and uses "make camp" for yakhanu; "encamp" (LOGOS) is the standard rendering of khanah. (d) EMET preserves yishkon ("would dwell") for the cloud's action; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "settled," which captures the sense in more natural English. The Hebrew shakhan ("to dwell/tabernacle") has theological resonance (root of Shekhinah), but in context "settled" conveys the meaning. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 9:18 -- "At the mouth of YHWH" vs. "Upon the mouth of YHWH" vs. "At YHWH's command" / "would set out" vs. "would journey" / "rested" vs. "would dwell" (al pi YHWH ... yis'u ... yakhanu ... yishkon)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Upon the-mouth of YHWH would-journey the-Sons of Israel, and-upon the-mouth of YHWH they-would-encamp; all the-days which the-cloud would-dwell upon the-Tabernacle they-would-encamp." |
| LOGOS | "At the mouth of YHWH the Sons of Israel would set out, and at the mouth of YHWH they would encamp. All the days that the cloud rested upon the tabernacle they would remain encamped." |
| PNEUMA | "At YHWH's command the Sons of Israel would set out, and at YHWH's command they would make camp. As long as the cloud rested over the Tabernacle, they remained in camp." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "At the mouth of YHWH the Sons of Israel would set out, and at the mouth of YHWH they would encamp. All the days that the cloud rested upon the Tabernacle, they would remain encamped." -- (a) al pi YHWH: This is the most significant recurring disagreement in vv. 18-23. EMET renders "upon the mouth of YHWH," LOGOS "at the mouth of YHWH," PNEUMA "at YHWH's command." The Hebrew pi means "mouth," and the phrase al pi means literally "upon/at the mouth of." LOGOS preserves the anthropomorphic image. PNEUMA interprets the idiom as "command," following the LXX's dia prostagmatos kyriou ("through the command of the Lord"). While PNEUMA's rendering captures the functional meaning, the Hebrew deliberately uses "mouth" (pi), and the consensus preserves this image per the principle of retaining Hebrew idiom where comprehensible. (b) PNEUMA uses "As long as" for kol yemei asher ("all the days that") and "remained in camp" for yakhanu ("encamped"). The consensus follows LOGOS with Tabernacle capitalized.

---

### 9:19 -- "when the cloud lingered" vs. "in the prolonging the cloud" vs. "Even when the cloud lingered" / "kept the charge" vs. "kept YHWH's charge" (u-ve-ha'arikh he-anan ... ve-shameru ... mishmeret YHWH)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-in-the-prolonging the-cloud upon the-Tabernacle days many, and-kept the-Sons of Israel the-charge of YHWH and-not they-journeyed." |
| LOGOS | "And when the cloud lingered upon the tabernacle many days, then the Sons of Israel kept the charge of YHWH and did not set out." |
| PNEUMA | "Even when the cloud lingered over the Tabernacle for many days, the Sons of Israel kept YHWH's charge and did not set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when the cloud lingered upon the Tabernacle many days, the Sons of Israel kept the charge of YHWH and did not set out." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "Even when" instead of "And when" -- the Hebrew u-ve-ha'arikh does not contain an emphatic particle; "Even" is an interpretive addition. (b) PNEUMA uses "over" for al; LOGOS uses "upon." Both are valid for al, but "upon" is used consistently throughout this section. (c) PNEUMA adds "for" before "many days" (not in Hebrew; the Hebrew is simply yamim rabbim). (d) EMET's "in the prolonging" is hyper-literal for the hiphil infinitive construct. LOGOS's "when the cloud lingered" is idiomatic. (e) LOGOS uses "then" -- not in the Hebrew; the consensus omits it. (f) Word order of "the charge of YHWH": EMET/LOGOS use "the charge of YHWH" (mishmeret YHWH), PNEUMA uses "YHWH's charge" (possessive restructuring). The consensus preserves the construct chain order. The consensus follows LOGOS with minor adjustment.

---

### 9:20 -- "And sometimes" vs. "And there was when" / "a number of days" vs. "days of number" vs. "only a few days" (ve-yesh asher ... yamim mispar)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-there-was when the-cloud would-be days of-number upon the-Tabernacle -- upon the-mouth of YHWH they-would-encamp and-upon the-mouth of YHWH they-would-journey." |
| LOGOS | "And sometimes the cloud was a number of days upon the tabernacle; according to the mouth of YHWH they would remain encamped, and according to the mouth of YHWH they would set out." |
| PNEUMA | "Sometimes the cloud remained over the Tabernacle for only a few days. At YHWH's command they would camp, and at YHWH's command they would set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And sometimes the cloud would be a number of days upon the Tabernacle; at the mouth of YHWH they would encamp, and at the mouth of YHWH they would set out." -- (a) ve-yesh asher: EMET's "And there was when" is hyper-literal; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "And sometimes" / "Sometimes." PNEUMA omits "And." (b) yamim mispar: EMET "days of number," LOGOS "a number of days," PNEUMA "only a few days." PNEUMA adds "only" (not in Hebrew) and interprets mispar as "few." The Hebrew means "a countable number of days" -- few, but the emphasis is on countability rather than fewness. LOGOS's "a number of days" captures this. (c) LOGOS uses "according to the mouth" instead of "at the mouth" -- the Hebrew al pi is the same phrase as v. 18. The consensus standardizes to "at the mouth of YHWH." (d) PNEUMA again renders al pi YHWH as "at YHWH's command." See note at v. 18.

---

### 9:21 -- "And sometimes" vs. "And there was when" / "or for a day and a night" vs. "or a day and a night" / "would be taken up" vs. "would go up" vs. "lifted" (ve-yesh asher ... ve-na'alah ... ve-nasa'u)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-there-was when the-cloud would-be from-evening until morning, and-the-cloud would-go-up in-the-morning, and-they-journeyed; or a-day and-a-night, and-the-cloud would-go-up, and-they-journeyed." |
| LOGOS | "And sometimes the cloud was from evening until morning, and the cloud was taken up in the morning, and they would set out; or a day and a night, and the cloud was taken up, and they would set out." |
| PNEUMA | "Sometimes the cloud stayed only from evening until morning. When the cloud lifted in the morning, they set out. Whether by day or by night, whenever the cloud lifted, they set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And sometimes the cloud would be from evening until morning, and the cloud would be taken up in the morning, and they would set out; or for a day and a night, and the cloud would be taken up, and they would set out." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" at the opening, adds "only" (not in Hebrew), restructures into three sentences, and fundamentally reinterprets the second half of the verse. The MT says "or [for] a day and a night, and the cloud would go up, and they journeyed" -- describing an alternative duration. PNEUMA renders this as "Whether by day or by night, whenever the cloud lifted, they set out," which is an entirely different construction that loses the "day and a night" duration scenario. (b) ve-na'alah: EMET "would go up," LOGOS "was taken up." The niphal of alah can be either "went up" or "was taken up." LOGOS's passive "was taken up" is slightly more interpretive but captures the divine agency. (c) EMET uses "they journeyed" (simple past); LOGOS uses "they would set out" (habitual). The imperfect tense in context conveys habitual action. The consensus follows LOGOS with "would be taken up" for consistent tense and adds "for" before "a day and a night" for smoother English.

---

### 9:22 -- "a longer time" vs. "days" vs. "an extended period" / "when the cloud lingered" vs. "in the prolonging the cloud" / "remaining upon it" vs. "to dwell upon it" vs. "resting upon it" (yomayim o chodesh o yamim / be-ha'arikh ... lishkon alav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Or two-days or a-month or days -- in-the-prolonging the-cloud upon the-Tabernacle to-dwell upon-it -- they-would-encamp the-Sons of Israel and-not they-would-journey; and-in-its-being-taken-up, they-would-journey." |
| LOGOS | "Whether it was two days, or a month, or a longer time, when the cloud lingered upon the tabernacle, remaining upon it, the Sons of Israel would remain encamped and would not set out; but when it was taken up, they would set out." |
| PNEUMA | "Whether for two days, or a month, or an extended period -- as long as the cloud lingered over the Tabernacle, resting upon it, the Sons of Israel remained in camp and did not set out. But when it lifted, they set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Whether it was two days, or a month, or a longer time -- when the cloud lingered upon the Tabernacle, remaining upon it -- the Sons of Israel would remain encamped and would not set out; but when it was taken up, they would set out." -- (a) yamim: EMET "days" (literal), LOGOS "a longer time" (contextual), PNEUMA "an extended period." The Hebrew yamim in this ascending sequence (two days ... a month ... yamim) means an indefinite but extended period. The LXX renders eniaution ("a year"), suggesting an ancient tradition of reading yamim as a long duration. "A longer time" (LOGOS) captures this progression without importing the LXX's specific "year." (b) lishkon alav: EMET "to dwell upon it," LOGOS "remaining upon it," PNEUMA "resting upon it." The Hebrew infinitive lishkon ("to dwell") has theological resonance (root of Shekhinah), but in context describes the cloud's continuing presence. "Remaining" is a natural English rendering. (c) PNEUMA uses "as long as" (interpretive), "remained in camp" (past tense rather than habitual), and "when it lifted" (omitting the niphal passive). The consensus follows LOGOS with capitalization.

---

### 9:23 -- "At the mouth of YHWH" vs. "At YHWH's command" / "they kept the charge of YHWH" vs. "They kept YHWH's charge" / "at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses" vs. "according to YHWH's command through Moses" (al pi YHWH ... mishmeret YHWH shamaru ... al pi YHWH be-yad Mosheh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Upon the-mouth of YHWH they-would-encamp and-upon the-mouth of YHWH they-would-journey; the-charge of YHWH they-kept, upon the-mouth of YHWH by-the-hand of Moses." |
| LOGOS | "At the mouth of YHWH they would encamp, and at the mouth of YHWH they would set out. They kept the charge of YHWH, at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses." |
| PNEUMA | "At YHWH's command they camped, and at YHWH's command they set out. They kept YHWH's charge, according to YHWH's command through Moses." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "At the mouth of YHWH they would encamp, and at the mouth of YHWH they would set out. They kept the charge of YHWH, at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses." -- (a) al pi YHWH: Same principle as v. 18. "At the mouth of YHWH" preserves the Hebrew anthropomorphism. PNEUMA renders all three occurrences as "at YHWH's command" / "according to YHWH's command," losing the powerful threefold repetition of "mouth." (b) PNEUMA uses "through Moses" for be-yad Mosheh ("by the hand of Moses"). The Hebrew be-yad means "by the hand of" and is a standard idiom for prophetic agency. "By the hand of" preserves this idiom. (c) PNEUMA uses simple past tense ("they camped," "they set out") rather than habitual ("they would encamp," "they would set out"). The imperfect verbs in context express habitual/repeated action throughout the wilderness period. (d) EMET uses "upon the mouth" for al pi; LOGOS/consensus use "at the mouth," which is more natural in English while still preserving the anthropomorphism. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Recurring Disagreements

**1. "celebrate" vs. "keep/make" for asah ha-Pesakh (vv. 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14):** PNEUMA consistently renders asah as "celebrate" throughout the Passover section. The Hebrew asah means "to make/do" and in cultic context means "to perform/observe/keep." "Celebrate" imports a festive connotation not inherent in the verb. The consensus uses "keep" throughout, following LOGOS.

**2. "at YHWH's command" vs. "at the mouth of YHWH" for al pi YHWH (vv. 18, 20, 23):** PNEUMA consistently interprets the anthropomorphic idiom, rendering pi ("mouth") as "command." The consensus preserves "at the mouth of YHWH" throughout, following EMET/LOGOS, because the Hebrew deliberately uses the body-part metaphor and its threefold repetition in v. 23 is a powerful rhetorical feature.

**3. Opening conjunction "And" (vv. 1, 4, 6, 9, 15, 17, 19, 20, 21):** PNEUMA frequently omits the opening "And" that renders the Hebrew vav-consecutive. Per binding convention, conjunctions are preserved, and the consensus retains "And" at verse openings throughout.

**4. "between the evenings" vs. "at twilight" for bein ha-arbayim (vv. 3, 5, 11):** EMET preserves the literal dual form. LOGOS/PNEUMA use "at twilight." The consensus follows "at twilight" as it captures the temporal sense without resolving the ancient exegetical dispute.

**5. "sojourner" vs. "stranger" vs. "foreigner" for ger (v. 14):** EMET uses "stranger," LOGOS "sojourner," PNEUMA "foreigner." The consensus uses "sojourner" as it preserves the etymological link to gur ("to sojourn") and is the traditional rendering of this important legal category.
